basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: al-Qamar — “The Moon” — opens upon the wonder: “The Hour has drawn near and the moon has split apart!” Qushayrī recalls that this miracle took place in the Prophet's time, and that Ibn Masʿūd could say, “I saw Mount Ḥirāʾ between the two halves of the moon.” The sūra makes of this sign the herald of the imminent Hour, then unfolds the procession of peoples destroyed for crying lies — Noah, ʿĀd, Thamūd, the people of Lot, the folk of Pharaoh. Two refrains punctuate it: “How [terrible] was My punishment and My warning!” and “We have made the Qur'an easy for remembrance (dhikr): is there any who will remember?” From the latter Qushayrī draws a pearl: God has made easy, for chosen hearts, the recitation, the knowledge, the understanding and the preserving of His Book — and all of them are the people of God and His elect. At the close of the parade of ruins, the sūra is sealed upon the dwelling of the godfearing: gardens and rivers, “in a seat of truth, with an Omnipotent King” — an intimate nearness, says Qushayrī, where the truthful servant no longer worships while eyeing reward.
themes: the imminent Hour and the split moon
the refrain of punishment and warning
the Qur'an made easy for dhikr
the seat of truth with the King

versets.ar: اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ وَانشَقَّ الْقَمَرُ
versets.tr: iqtarabati s-sāʿatu wa-nshaqqa l-qamaru
versets.trad: The Hour has drawn near and the moon has split apart!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī recalls that the commentators agree on the reality of the miracle: in the Prophet's time the moon truly split in two, so that Ibn Masʿūd could say, “I saw Mount Ḥirāʾ between the two halves of the moon.” He sees in it a twofold wonder: the vision of those who saw it, and the hiddenness of such a sign from those who did not — for what cannot normally pass unnoticed, by remaining veiled, breaks the customary order. As for “the Hour has drawn near,” he explains: of all the time remaining until the Resurrection, only a little is left, compared with what has elapsed.

versets.ar: وَإِن يَرَوْا آيَةً يُعْرِضُوا وَيَقُولُوا سِحْرٌ مُّسْتَمِرٌّ
versets.tr: wa-in yaraw āyatan yuʿriḍū wa-yaqūlū siḥrun mustamirrun
versets.trad: Yet if they see a sign they turn away and say, “A persistent magic!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī observes that when the people of Mecca saw one of the signs, they turned away from all reflection; yet had they but looked, knowledge would necessarily have been forced upon them. As for “a persistent magic,” he reports two senses: a strong, lasting magic; or, conversely, a passing magic that will not endure. To avert the gaze from a sign is to deprive oneself of the very light it carried.

mots.racine: س ح ر / م ر ر
mots.def: siḥr: magic; mustamirr: continuous, or passing.

versets.ar: وَكَذَّبُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا أَهْوَاءَهُمْ وَكُلُّ أَمْرٍ مُّسْتَقِرٌّ
versets.tr: wa-kadhdhabū wa-ttabaʿū ahwāʾahum wa-kullu amrin mustaqirrun
versets.trad: They cried lies and followed their own desires; but every matter shall reach its settled term.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws the two evils together: denial and the following of desire are neighbours; once one follows one's whim, denial follows from its ill omen, for God veils such a heart until it can no longer discern right guidance. Conversely, he says, following God's good pleasure is bound to faithful assent (taṣdīq), for through the blessing of seeking the Real, God opens the eye of inner vision and belief takes root. And “every matter is settled”: the believer's deed is settled and earns him the Garden; the denier's deed is settled and he is requited.

mots.racine: أ م ر / ق ر ر
mots.def: mustaqirr: that comes to rest, reaches its term.

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ جَاءَهُم مِّنَ الْأَنبَاءِ مَا فِيهِ مُزْدَجَرٌ
versets.tr: wa-laqad jāʾahum mina l-anbāʾi mā fīhi muzdajarun
versets.trad: There has come to them of the tidings that wherein is deterrence,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: there came to them the accounts of the prophets and the earlier peoples, and of ages past, enough to impose the curb of repentance; but the Real drew over their inner sight the veils of ignorance, and they were blind to the places of right guidance. The warning sufficed; it was the eye of the heart that withdrew.

mots.sens: there has indeed come to them

versets.ar: حِكْمَةٌ بَالِغَةٌ فَمَا تُغْنِ النُّذُرُ
versets.tr: ḥikmatun bālighatun fa-mā tughni n-nudhuru
versets.trad: a far-reaching wisdom; but the warnings avail nothing.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes that “consummate wisdom” stands in apposition to what precedes: the perfected wisdom is that which is sound, manifest, evident to whoever ponders it. And before such hardness he exclaims: what use is the warning of one who warns, when the decree of wretchedness has already been traced out for them? The wisdom is whole; it is the closed heart that nullifies its fruit.

mots.racine: ح ك م / ب ل غ
mots.def: ḥikma: wisdom; bāligha: reaching its aim.

versets.ar: فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ يَوْمَ يَدْعُ الدَّاعِ إِلَىٰ شَيْءٍ نُّكُرٍ
versets.tr: fa-tawalla ʿanhum yawma yadʿu d-dāʿi ilā shayʾin nukurin
versets.trad: So turn away from them. On the Day the Caller shall call to a dreadful thing,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī marks a pause here: “turn away from them” — the sense ends there: withdraw from them; and this was said before the command to fight. Then the discourse resumes upon the Day: “the Day the Caller shall call” means the Resurrection. And “a dreadful thing (nukur)”: a thing recoiled from for its horror and ghastliness; it is the Day of the Gathering.

mots.sens: the Day the Caller shall call
mots.def: dāʿī: the one who summons (the angel).
nukur: horrifying, unrecognizable in its terror.

versets.ar: خُشَّعًا أَبْصَارُهُمْ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ الْأَجْدَاثِ كَأَنَّهُمْ جَرَادٌ مُّنتَشِرٌ
versets.tr: khushshaʿan abṣāruhum yakhrujūna mina l-ajdāthi ka-annahum jarādun muntashirun
versets.trad: their eyes downcast, they will come forth from the graves as though they were scattered locusts,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī explains that “downcast eyes (khushshaʿan)” is a circumstantial state: they come forth from the graves — the ajdāth — with the eye humbled and lowered. And the likeness to scattered locusts holds, he says, because of their multitude and their dispersion. The gaze once averted from the signs now comes forth bent low under the weight of evidence.

mots.racine: خ ش ع / ب ص ر
ج ر د / ن ش ر

versets.ar: مُّهْطِعِينَ إِلَى الدَّاعِ يَقُولُ الْكَافِرُونَ هَٰذَا يَوْمٌ عَسِرٌ
versets.tr: muhṭiʿīna ilā d-dāʿi yaqūlu l-kāfirūna hādhā yawmun ʿasirun
versets.trad: hastening with necks outstretched toward the Caller. The deniers will say, “This is a hard Day!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they are like locusts in their number; “muhṭiʿīn” means: with their gaze fixed unwaveringly upon the Caller — who is Isrāfīl. And the deniers cry, “This is a hard Day,” because of the unbroken succession of hardships it contains. The hardness lies not in the Day but in the heart that had prepared no dwelling for it.

mots.def: muhṭiʿ: rushing with neck outstretched, eyes fixed.

mots.racine: ق و ل / ك ف ر

versets.ar: كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ فَكَذَّبُوا عَبْدَنَا وَقَالُوا مَجْنُونٌ وَازْدُجِرَ
versets.tr: kadhdhabat qablahum qawmu nūḥin fa-kadhdhabū ʿabdanā wa-qālū majnūnun wa-zdujira
versets.trad: Before them the people of Noah cried lies. They denied Our servant and said, “A man possessed!”, and he was rebuffed.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the people of Noah denied their prophet, called him possessed, rebuffed and reviled him. As for “wa-zdujira,” he also reports: his mind was driven astray — that is, the people of Noah claimed this of him. The faithful servant is here named “Our servant”: a title of honour upon the very lips of the One whom they were insulting.

mots.def: izdujira: to be driven off, rebuffed.

versets.ar: فَدَعَا رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ فَانتَصِرْ
versets.tr: fa-daʿā rabbahu annī maghlūbun fa-ntaṣir
versets.trad: So he called upon his Lord, “I am overcome; give victory!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: he called upon his Lord saying, “I am overcome” — that is, by my people's domination over me; not overcome in argument, for the proof was against them; and Noah said to God, “O Lord, give me victory over them” — that is, take vengeance. The defeat was not that of truth, but the outward crushing of the servant, who casts himself wholly upon his Master.

mots.racine: د ع و / ر ب ب

versets.ar: فَفَتَحْنَا أَبْوَابَ السَّمَاءِ بِمَاءٍ مُّنْهَمِرٍ
versets.tr: fa-fataḥnā abwāba s-samāʾi bi-māʾin munhamirin
versets.trad: So We opened the gates of heaven with torrential water,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We opened the gates of heaven to a pouring water. The aid of heaven answers exactly the prayer of the oppressed servant: the door he had knocked upon by supplication opens in cataracts. What in man was a humble call becomes in God a deluge of response.

mots.racine: ب و ب / س م و
م و ه / ه م ر

versets.ar: وَفَجَّرْنَا الْأَرْضَ عُيُونًا فَالْتَقَى الْمَاءُ عَلَىٰ أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ
versets.tr: wa-fajjarnā l-arḍa ʿuyūnan fa-ltaqā l-māʾu ʿalā amrin qad qudira
versets.trad: and We made the earth burst forth with springs, and the waters met for a matter already decreed.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We cleft springs of water, and the water of heaven and the water of earth met according to a decree already fixed in the Preserved Tablet, ordained for their destruction. The meeting of the two waters is nothing fortuitous: it is the punctual execution of an order inscribed before all time.

mots.racine: ف ج ر / أ ر ض
mots.def: fajjara: to cause to burst forth; arḍ: earth.

mots.racine: أ م ر / ق د ر

versets.ar: وَحَمَلْنَاهُ عَلَىٰ ذَاتِ أَلْوَاحٍ وَدُسُرٍ
versets.tr: wa-ḥamalnāhu ʿalā dhāti alwāḥin wa-dusurin
versets.trad: And We carried him upon a vessel of planks and nails,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We carried Noah upon “the one of planks” — that is, the ark — “and dusur”: the nails, plural of disār, a nail. The oppressed servant, who had only his complaint, is now borne aloft: God does not merely answer, He lifts up and saves the one who entrusted himself to Him.

mots.def: dusur: nails, or fibres binding the planks.

versets.ar: تَجْرِي بِأَعْيُنِنَا جَزَاءً لِّمَن كَانَ كُفِرَ
versets.tr: tajrī bi-aʿyuninā jazāʾan li-man kāna kufira
versets.trad: running before Our eyes — a reward for him who had been rejected,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī opens the phrase “before Our eyes” to several senses: under Our watch; and it is said: by Our friends; and it is said: under the eyes of Our angels whom We appointed to guard them; and it is said: by the eyes of the water We caused to gush from the face of the earth. And “for him who had been rejected”: those who disbelieved in Noah — sailing under the very gaze of the One they had denied.

mots.def: kufira: to be denied, ungratefully rejected.

versets.ar: وَلَقَد تَّرَكْنَاهَا آيَةً فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ
versets.tr: wa-laqad taraknāhā āyatan fa-hal min muddakirin
versets.trad: and We left it as a sign. Is there, then, any who will remember?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We made the matter of the ark a clear token for whoever takes heed. And “is there any who will remember?”: is there among you one who takes the lesson? — the question here is a command to ponder. The sign abides; what remains is to find the heart willing to be instructed by it.

versets.ar: فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ
versets.tr: fa-kayfa kāna ʿadhābī wa-nudhuri
versets.trad: How [terrible] was My punishment and My warning!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes that this is said by way of magnifying the matter. He also observes that the story of Noah is here given in the most eloquent expression, the briefest speech, the fullest meaning. The refrain is no idle threat: it seals the tale of Noah by raising the solemnity of the warning, calling the heart to measure the gravity of what came to pass.

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ
versets.tr: wa-laqad yassarnā l-qurʾāna li-dh-dhikri fa-hal min muddakirin
versets.trad: And We have indeed made the Qur'an easy for remembrance. Is there, then, any who will remember?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds this “making easy” in degrees: We made its recitation easy upon the tongues of men; We made its knowledge easy for the hearts of one folk; We made its understanding easy for the hearts of another; and We made its memorization easy for the hearts of yet another — and all of them are the people of the Qur'an, all of them the people of God and His elect. He even adds: He unveiled the Qur'an to certain spirits before bringing them into bodies. “Is there any who will remember” — this ancient covenant once made with Him?

versets.ar: كَذَّبَتْ عَادٌ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ
versets.tr: kadhdhabat ʿādun fa-kayfa kāna ʿadhābī wa-nudhuri
versets.trad: ʿĀd cried lies; how [terrible] was My punishment and My warning!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they denied Hūd. The refrain returns, but upon a new people: the same formula, applied to each rebellious generation, gathers an ever-deepening gravity. What struck Noah strikes the ʿĀd in turn; the warning has not changed, only those who neglect it.

mots.def: ʿĀd: people of the prophet Hūd.

versets.ar: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا فِي يَوْمِ نَحْسٍ مُّسْتَمِرٍّ
versets.tr: innā arsalnā ʿalayhim rīḥan ṣarṣaran fī yawmi naḥsin mustamirrin
versets.trad: We loosed against them a howling wind on a day of unrelenting calamity,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We sent upon them a “rīḥ ṣarṣar”: an icy wind, violent of blast, roaring. And “on a day of persistent ill-fortune”: a day of evil omen in which the punishment pressed upon them and lasted — eight days and seven nights; and it is said: of unceasing ill-fortune. The wind that once raised the sands becomes the exact instrument of justice.

mots.racine: ر و ح / ص ر ر
ن ح س / م ر ر
mots.sens: on a day of persistent calamity

versets.ar: تَنزِعُ النَّاسَ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجَازُ نَخْلٍ مُّنقَعِرٍ
versets.tr: tanziʿu n-nāsa ka-annahum aʿjāzu nakhlin munqaʿirin
versets.trad: tearing out men as though they were uprooted trunks of palm-trees.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the wind tore the people from the pits they had dug, until it left them like trunks of broken palm-trees; and it is said: it carried off their heads from their shoulders, then cast them down like palm-stumps with their crowns severed. An image of human frailty when the decree is unleashed: the proud man is but a hollow trunk swept away.

mots.racine: ع ج ز / ن خ ل / ق ع ر
mots.def: ʿajuz: trunk-stump; munqaʿir: torn from the ground.

versets.ar: فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ
versets.tr: fa-kayfa kāna ʿadhābī wa-nudhuri
versets.trad: How [terrible] was My punishment and My warning!
versets.commentaire: The refrain seals the ruin of ʿĀd. By way of magnification, says Qushayrī, it compels the heart to contemplate the terrible grandeur of the divine act. Repeated, it does not weary: each return digs deeper the call to take heed, like a bell tolling from age to age over the peoples who did not hear.

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ
versets.tr: wa-laqad yassarnā l-qurʾāna li-dh-dhikri fa-hal min muddakirin
versets.trad: And We have indeed made the Qur'an easy for remembrance. Is there, then, any who will remember?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī underscores here a singularity of the Book: We have lightened its recitation and its memorization; none of God's Books is recited from memory, by heart, save the Qur'an. Here is the miracle of its facilitation: this immense Book, God has lodged whole in the breast of whomever He loves. There remains the call: “Is there any who will remember?”

mots.racine: ق ر أ / ذ ك ر

versets.ar: كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِالنُّذُرِ
versets.tr: kadhdhabat thamūdu bi-n-nudhuri
versets.trad: Thamūd cried lies against the warnings.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī recalls that this is the people of Ṣāliḥ, whose story has already been told, with the episode in which they hamstrung the she-camel, until God sent upon them a single cry that brought their ruin, leaving them like withered chaff. To deny the warner is to doom oneself to the cry that does not pardon.

mots.def: Thamūd: people of the prophet Ṣāliḥ.

versets.ar: فَقَالُوا أَبَشَرًا مِّنَّا وَاحِدًا نَّتَّبِعُهُ إِنَّا إِذًا لَّفِي ضَلَالٍ وَسُعُرٍ
versets.tr: fa-qālū a-basharan minnā wāḥidan nattabiʿuhu innā idhan la-fī ḍalālin wa-suʿurin
versets.trad: They said, “Are we to follow a single mortal from among ourselves? Then we would be in error and madness.
versets.commentaire: The pride of Thamūd is laid bare in their refusal to follow “a single mortal from among us”: their hearts recoiled at the thought that a man like themselves should be chosen. They name the very path of salvation “error and madness.” Such is the reversal in the denying heart: it takes guidance for derangement, and its own derangement for reason.

mots.racine: ب ش ر / و ح د
ض ل ل / س ع ر
mots.sens: we would be in error and madness

versets.ar: أَؤُلْقِيَ الذِّكْرُ عَلَيْهِ مِن بَيْنِنَا بَلْ هُوَ كَذَّابٌ أَشِرٌ
versets.tr: a-ulqiya dh-dhikru ʿalayhi min bayninā bal huwa kadhdhābun ashirun
versets.trad: Has the Reminder been cast upon him alone from among us? No — he is an insolent liar!”
versets.commentaire: The scandal of Thamūd is that of election: why him, and not one of us? They cannot bear that the divine gift should alight where it wills. So they call him “an insolent liar” — for the envious heart, unable to acknowledge grace in another, recasts it as imposture. The true madness lies there, not in the chosen one.

mots.racine: ل ق ي / ذ ك ر
mots.sens: has the Reminder been cast upon him?
mots.def: ulqiya: to be cast, laid; dhikr: the message.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب / أ ش ر
mots.sens: no, he is an insolent liar

versets.ar: سَيَعْلَمُونَ غَدًا مَّنِ الْكَذَّابُ الْأَشِرُ
versets.tr: sa-yaʿlamūna ghadan mani l-kadhdhābu l-ashiru
versets.trad: Tomorrow they shall know who is the insolent liar!
versets.commentaire: To those who called the servant of God “an insolent liar,” the verse returns their very words: tomorrow — on the day of punishment and resurrection — they shall know who was truly the insolent liar. The divine “tomorrow” undoes all the hasty judgements of men: what they had overturned will be set right.

mots.racine: ع ل م / غ د و
ك ذ ب / أ ش ر

versets.ar: إِنَّا مُرْسِلُو النَّاقَةِ فِتْنَةً لَّهُمْ فَارْتَقِبْهُمْ وَاصْطَبِرْ
versets.tr: innā mursilū n-nāqati fitnatan lahum fa-rtaqibhum wa-ṣṭabir
versets.trad: We are sending the she-camel as a trial for them; so watch them and be patient.
versets.commentaire: The she-camel is sent as “fitna” — a trial that distinguishes hearts. To Ṣāliḥ, God commands to watch and to be patient: the prophet's twofold charge before a hardened people. Patience (ṣabr) is here the servant's weapon when the very sign becomes, for the rebellious, an occasion of downfall.

mots.racine: ر س ل / ن و ق

versets.ar: وَنَبِّئْهُمْ أَنَّ الْمَاءَ قِسْمَةٌ بَيْنَهُمْ كُلُّ شِرْبٍ مُّحْتَضَرٌ
versets.tr: wa-nabbiʾhum anna l-māʾa qismatun baynahum kullu shirbin muḥtaḍarun
versets.trad: And tell them that the water is to be shared between them, each drinking turn duly attended.
versets.commentaire: The sharing of the water between the she-camel and the people is a measure of divine equity, where each is “present at his turn of drinking.” The trial lodges itself even in the ordinary thirst: God tests hearts by the very apportioning of His bounty. To honour the turn is to acknowledge the appointed order; to transgress it is to summon the cry.

mots.racine: م و ه / ق س م
mots.sens: that the water is shared between them

mots.racine: ش ر ب / ح ض ر
mots.def: shirb: share of water; muḥtaḍar: attended in turn.

versets.ar: فَنَادَوْا صَاحِبَهُمْ فَتَعَاطَىٰ فَعَقَرَ
versets.tr: fa-nādaw ṣāḥibahum fa-taʿāṭā fa-ʿaqara
versets.trad: But they called their companion, and he took up the blade and hamstrung her.
versets.commentaire: The crime is knit in complicity: they called their companion, he took up his weapon and slaughtered the she-camel. The whole people is one in the act of a single man, for they had urged him on. To break God's sign with one's own hand is to consummate the denial that until then had lived only on the lips.

mots.racine: ن د و / ص ح ب

mots.def: taʿāṭā: to take in hand, undertake.

versets.ar: فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ
versets.tr: fa-kayfa kāna ʿadhābī wa-nudhuri
versets.trad: How [terrible] was My punishment and My warning!
versets.commentaire: The refrain falls upon Thamūd after the crime of the she-camel. A magnifying of the divine matter, says Qushayrī: the formula that sealed Noah and then ʿĀd here seals a third people, and its very insistence declares that God never warns in vain. Whoever breaks the sign has already heard, without heeding, the thunder that comes.

versets.ar: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ صَيْحَةً وَاحِدَةً فَكَانُوا كَهَشِيمِ الْمُحْتَظِرِ
versets.tr: innā arsalnā ʿalayhim ṣayḥatan wāḥidatan fa-kānū ka-hashīmi l-muḥtaẓiri
versets.trad: We sent against them a single cry, and they became like the dry stubble of a pen.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God sent upon them a single cry that sufficed for their ruin, and rendered them like the hashīm — withered chaff. As for “al-muḥtaẓir,” he notes the twofold reading: that which is placed in the pen, or that which is gathered there. One cry suffices: omnipotence has no need to repeat where man piles up his strength in vain.

mots.racine: ص ي ح / و ح د
ه ش م / ح ظ ر
mots.sens: they became like the chaff of the pen
mots.def: hashīm: broken dry grass; muḥtaẓir: pen, enclosure.

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ
versets.tr: wa-laqad yassarnā l-qurʾāna li-dh-dhikri fa-hal min muddakirin
versets.trad: And We have indeed made the Qur'an easy for remembrance. Is there, then, any who will remember?
versets.commentaire: After the ruin of each people, the same call returns, like a hand held out. God has made easy for the believer's heart the reading, the knowledge, the understanding and the preserving of His Book: so many doors opened to escape the fate of the deniers. Amid the tale of punishments, the refrain reminds us that the remedy is there, easy, within reach of the heart — “Is there any who will remember?”

mots.racine: ي س ر / ق ر أ
mots.sens: We have made the Qur'an easy

versets.ar: كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ بِالنُّذُرِ
versets.tr: kadhdhabat qawmu lūṭin bi-n-nudhuri
versets.trad: The people of Lot cried lies against the warnings.
versets.commentaire: The denial repeats, true to itself, in the people of Lot. The same sickness of heart that struck Noah, ʿĀd and Thamūd reappears: to refuse the warning is to blind oneself to the light it bore. Each people thinks its case unique, yet it is always the same refusal that prepares the same ruin.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب / ق و م

versets.ar: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حَاصِبًا إِلَّا آلَ لُوطٍ نَّجَّيْنَاهُم بِسَحَرٍ
versets.tr: innā arsalnā ʿalayhim ḥāṣiban illā āla lūṭin najjaynāhum bi-saḥarin
versets.trad: We loosed against them a storm of stones, save the family of Lot, whom We delivered at dawn,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We sent upon them a “ḥāṣib”: stones with which they were pelted. But the family of Lot is saved “at dawn”: at the very heart of the night of punishment, an outlet of light is appointed for the faithful. The same hour that seals the ruin of some becomes the dawn of salvation for others.

mots.racine: ر س ل / ح ص ب
mots.sens: We sent upon them a hail of stones

mots.racine: ن ج و / س ح ر
mots.def: najjā: to save; saḥar: last part of the night.

versets.ar: نِّعْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي مَن شَكَرَ
versets.tr: niʿmatan min ʿindinā kadhālika najzī man shakara
versets.trad: as a grace from Us. Thus do We reward whoever gives thanks.
versets.commentaire: From this verse Qushayrī draws a pearl: “Thus do We reward whoever gives thanks” — We placed their salvation within the very ruin of their enemies; thus do We deal with whoever gives thanks for Our bounty. And he adds this subtlety: thanksgiving for the graces of preservation (evil averted) is more perfect than thanksgiving for the graces of benefit received — and none knows this save every aided, discerning soul.

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ أَنذَرَهُم بَطْشَتَنَا فَتَمَارَوْا بِالنُّذُرِ
versets.tr: wa-laqad andharahum baṭshatanā fa-tamāraw bi-n-nudhuri
versets.trad: He had warned them of Our grip, but they disputed the warnings.
versets.commentaire: Lot had warned them of “Our grip” — the sudden seizing of God —, but they disputed and cast the warnings into doubt. Such is the mainspring of the perishing heart: not ignorance, but dispute; it has heard, it has even understood, and it is through deliberate contestation that it ruins itself.

mots.racine: م ر ي / ن ذ ر

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ رَاوَدُوهُ عَن ضَيْفِهِ فَطَمَسْنَا أَعْيُنَهُمْ فَذُوقُوا عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ
versets.tr: wa-laqad rāwadūhu ʿan ḍayfihi fa-ṭamasnā aʿyunahum fa-dhūqū ʿadhābī wa-nudhuri
versets.trad: They even sought to seize his guests, so We blotted out their eyes: “Taste, then, My punishment and My warning!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports that Gabriel passed his wing over their faces and they were blinded, unable to find the way out. And from this he draws a law of the divine gaze: such is His custom toward His friends — He blinds the hearts of their enemies, veils from them the means of harming the saints, then delivers them from their plotting. To assail the guest of God is to lose even one's sight.

mots.racine: ر و د / ض ي ف
mots.sens: they sought to seize his guests
mots.def: rāwada: to solicit insistently; ḍayf: guest.

mots.racine: ط م س / ع ي ن
ذ و ق / ع ذ ب

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ صَبَّحَهُم بُكْرَةً عَذَابٌ مُّسْتَقِرٌّ
versets.tr: wa-laqad ṣabbaḥahum bukratan ʿadhābun mustaqirrun
versets.trad: And at early dawn an abiding punishment overtook them.
versets.commentaire: The punishment surprises them “in the morning”: the hour when sleep is deepest, when the heedless deems himself most secure. And it is “mustaqirr” — abiding, that does not pass. When the decree is fulfilled, it knows neither delay nor respite: what was foretold settles permanently in place.

mots.racine: ص ب ح / ب ك ر
mots.def: ṣabbaḥa: to come in the morning; bukra: dawn.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / ق ر ر

versets.ar: فَذُوقُوا عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ
versets.tr: fa-dhūqū ʿadhābī wa-nudhuri
versets.trad: So taste My punishment and My warning!
versets.commentaire: The refrain here takes the form of “taste”: no longer merely “how was My punishment,” but the command to taste it in the flesh. What they refused to hear as a word, they taste as torment. The warning unreceived in speech becomes a bitter savour: it was not lost, it returns as punishment.

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ
versets.tr: wa-laqad yassarnā l-qurʾāna li-dh-dhikri fa-hal min muddakirin
versets.trad: And We have indeed made the Qur'an easy for remembrance. Is there, then, any who will remember?
versets.commentaire: The fourth return of the refrain, after the ruin of the people of Lot. Its very repetition is a mercy: as often as the tale of perdition is told, so often is the call to remembrance renewed. The eased Qur'an remains the one plank of salvation amid the deluges of the swallowed nations. At each reminder the door stays open: “Is there any who will remember?”

mots.racine: ي س ر / ق ر أ
mots.sens: We have made the Qur'an easy

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ جَاءَ آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ النُّذُرُ
versets.tr: wa-laqad jāʾa āla firʿawna n-nudhuru
versets.trad: And to the people of Pharaoh the warnings came.
versets.commentaire: To the people of Pharaoh too the warnings came: no people has been punished without first being warned. God's justice never strikes blindly; it sends first the admonition. That Pharaoh, summit of earthly pride, also received the warning proves that the door of repentance stays open even to the most hardened.

mots.def: āl Firʿawn: the house of Pharaoh.

versets.ar: كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا كُلِّهَا فَأَخَذْنَاهُمْ أَخْذَ عَزِيزٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍ
versets.tr: kadhdhabū bi-āyātinā kullihā fa-akhadhnāhum akhdha ʿazīzin muqtadirin
versets.trad: They denied all Our signs, so We seized them with the seizing of One Mighty, Omnipotent.
versets.commentaire: Having denied “all” the signs, they are seized “with the seizing of a Mighty, All-Capable One.” God's seizing is not like the grasp of creatures: it is the grip of the One whom nothing overflows, whose power knows neither flaw nor appeal. The more total the denial — “all Our signs” —, the more the seizing is beyond all recourse.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب / أ ي ي
ع ز ز / ق د ر
mots.sens: with the seizing of a Mighty, Capable One

versets.ar: أَكُفَّارُكُمْ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ أُولَٰئِكُمْ أَمْ لَكُم بَرَاءَةٌ فِي الزُّبُرِ
versets.tr: a-kuffārukum khayrun min ulāʾikum am lakum barāʾatun fī z-zuburi
versets.trad: Are your deniers better than those? Or have you some exemption in the Scriptures?
versets.commentaire: The address turns to the Prophet's contemporaries: are your deniers any better than those who were annihilated? Or do you possess some writ of immunity inscribed in the Books? None is exempt from the divine law: not ʿĀd, not Thamūd, not Pharaoh were so; and no decree of acquittal protects whoever persists in denial.

mots.racine: ك ف ر / خ ي ر
ب ر أ / ز ب ر
mots.sens: or have you exemption in the Scriptures?

versets.ar: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ نَحْنُ جَمِيعٌ مُّنتَصِرٌ
versets.tr: am yaqūlūna naḥnu jamīʿun muntaṣirun
versets.trad: Or do they say, “We are a host victorious”?
versets.commentaire: They delude themselves over their numbers: “We are a united and victorious host.” But multitude has never protected anyone from God: the annihilated peoples were more numerous and stronger. To trust in the group is to blind oneself; the only victory is the one that comes from on high, and it turns against the pride of those who deem themselves invincible.

mots.racine: ج م ع / ن ص ر

versets.ar: سَيُهْزَمُ الْجَمْعُ وَيُوَلُّونَ الدُّبُرَ
versets.tr: sa-yuhzamu l-jamʿu wa-yuwallūna d-dubura
versets.trad: The host shall be routed, and they shall turn their backs.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī recalls that this promise was fulfilled on the day of Badr, when the host of the Prophet's enemies was routed and turned their backs, and this became one of his miracles. The prediction of a victory, uttered when the believers were few and weak, came true to the letter: the “victorious host” was precisely the one that fled.

mots.racine: ه ز م / ج م ع
mots.def: huzima: to be put to flight.

mots.racine: و ل ي / د ب ر
mots.sens: and they shall turn their backs

versets.ar: بَلِ السَّاعَةُ مَوْعِدُهُمْ وَالسَّاعَةُ أَدْهَىٰ وَأَمَرُّ
versets.tr: bali s-sāʿatu mawʿiduhum wa-s-sāʿatu adhā wa-amarru
versets.trad: Nay, the Hour is their appointed time, and the Hour is more calamitous and more bitter.
versets.commentaire: The earthly defeat is but a foretaste: the true appointment is the Hour, more crushing and more bitter still. The sūra, opened upon “the Hour has drawn near,” returns to it: the whole procession of ancient punishments was preparing for this ultimate reckoning, of which the bygone ruins are but the heralding signs.

mots.racine: س و ع / و ع د
mots.sens: the Hour is their appointed time

mots.racine: د ه ي / م ر ر
mots.sens: the Hour is more terrible and bitter
mots.def: adhā: more calamitous; amarr: more bitter.

versets.ar: إِنَّ الْمُجْرِمِينَ فِي ضَلَالٍ وَسُعُرٍ
versets.tr: inna l-mujrimīna fī ḍalālin wa-suʿurin
versets.trad: Truly the sinners are in error and madness.
versets.commentaire: The very words of Thamūd — “error and madness” — are turned back upon them: what they ascribed to the path of guidance dwells in reality within their own hearts. The criminal already lives here below in ḍalāl and suʿur, error and the burning fire of passion, before finding them as a dwelling in the hereafter.

mots.racine: ض ل ل / س ع ر

versets.ar: يَوْمَ يُسْحَبُونَ فِي النَّارِ عَلَىٰ وُجُوهِهِمْ ذُوقُوا مَسَّ سَقَرَ
versets.tr: yawma yusḥabūna fī n-nāri ʿalā wujūhihim dhūqū massa saqara
versets.trad: The Day they are dragged on their faces into the Fire: “Taste the touch of Saqar!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: their being dragged on their faces is the mark of their abasement; and were it but once it would already be immense — what then of eternity and perpetuity? And he sets the two faces opposite: as the mark of humiliation appears on their faces, the mark of the believers' honour appears on theirs — “faces that Day shall be radiant,” “you shall know on their faces the radiance of bliss.”

mots.racine: س ح ب / ن و ر
mots.sens: the Day they are dragged into the Fire
mots.def: suḥiba: to be dragged; nār: fire.

mots.racine: ذ و ق / م س س / س ق ر
mots.def: saqar: a name of Hell; mass: touch, contact.

versets.ar: إِنَّا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنَاهُ بِقَدَرٍ
versets.tr: innā kulla shayʾin khalaqnāhu bi-qadarin
versets.trad: Truly We have created everything according to a measure.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We have created all things according to a measure inscribed in the Preserved Tablet; and it is said: according to the measure of Our knowledge, Our will and Our foretelling. Nothing in the universe escapes the qadar: neither the speck of dust nor the destiny of peoples. This measure is no blind constraint but the perfect wisdom of the One who knows, wills and ordains.

versets.ar: وَمَا أَمْرُنَا إِلَّا وَاحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ
versets.tr: wa-mā amrunā illā wāḥidatun ka-lamḥin bi-l-baṣari
versets.trad: And Our command is but a single word, like the twinkling of an eye.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: when We will to create a thing, it is neither difficult nor impossible for Us; We say to it “Be,” and it is. And he draws out the comparison: just as the twinkling of an eye costs you no effort, so, when We will to create — little or much, small or great —, no toil reaches Us. Omnipotence needs neither effort nor duration.

mots.racine: أ م ر / و ح د
ل م ح / ب ص ر
mots.sens: like the twinkling of an eye

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَا أَشْيَاعَكُمْ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ
versets.tr: wa-laqad ahlaknā ashyāʿakum fa-hal min muddakirin
versets.trad: We have destroyed the likes of you. Is there, then, any who will remember?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We have destroyed the generations that preceded you — all your likes among the children of Adam. The phrase “your likes” bespeaks the secret kinship among the rebels of every age: whoever denies today walks in the footsteps of yesterday's drowned. The call returns, urgent: “Is there any who will take the lesson?”

versets.ar: وَكُلُّ شَيْءٍ فَعَلُوهُ فِي الزُّبُرِ
versets.tr: wa-kullu shayʾin faʿalūhu fī z-zuburi
versets.trad: And everything they did is in the records,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: everything is inscribed in the Preserved Tablet even before it is done, and recorded too on the angels' scrolls; nothing, small or great, escapes that reckoning. Man's deed, fleeting in time, abides eternally inscribed: what the hand does, the divine pen has already retained.

versets.ar: وَكُلُّ صَغِيرٍ وَكَبِيرٍ مُّسْتَطَرٌ
versets.tr: wa-kullu ṣaghīrin wa-kabīrin mustaṭarun
versets.trad: and every matter, small and great, is set down.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: every deed, small or great, is written in the Preserved Tablet and in the angels' register. And he discloses its spiritual purpose: to make known to man what the angels record is an admonition, that the servant may not dare the sin, knowing he will be called to account for it. The awareness of being written awakens the heart's vigilance.

mots.racine: ص غ ر / ك ب ر

versets.ar: إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَنَهَرٍ
versets.tr: inna l-muttaqīna fī jannātin wa-naharin
versets.trad: Truly the godfearing shall be amid Gardens and rivers,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: theirs are gardens and rivers; and when a plural answers a plural, each single one answers a single one — so the apparent sense is that each of the godfearing has his own garden and his own river. After the long procession of punished peoples, the sūra opens at last upon the dwelling of the godfearing: rest, greenery and rivers, for those who knew how to take heed.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / ن ه ر

versets.ar: فِي مَقْعَدِ صِدْقٍ عِندَ مَلِيكٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍ
versets.tr: fī maqʿadi ṣidqin ʿinda malīkin muqtadirin
versets.trad: in a seat of truth, with an Omnipotent King.
versets.commentaire: The seal of the sūra is the summit of all: Qushayrī explains “with an Omnipotent King” by the nearness of approach and intimate closeness (ʿindiyyat al-qurba wa-z-zulfa). And he defines the “seat of truth”: the truthful one in his worship is he who does not worship while eyeing some craving nor watching for rewards. Then he ascends by degrees: whoever clings to this world is veiled from the hereafter; whoever is captivated by the delights of the Garden is veiled from the Reality; and whoever stands by the Reality is drawn away, absorbed beyond the whole of creation.

mots.racine: ق ع د / ص د ق
م ل ك / ق د ر
